coredata.dc:description: The production of high-value carbon materials via biomass pyrolysis has gained significant attention in recent research due to its efficiency and sustainability. Unlike the resource-intensive processes for obtaining carbon materials from fossil fuels, biomass pyrolysis provides an alternative that can utilize renewable and abundant sources at lower cost. Although biomass pyrolysis often produces a broad range of products, making precise control over specific carbon materials challenging, it offers flexibility in generating various high-value materials, including activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon foams, and carbon quantum dots. This review comprehensively examines the past decade's advancements in preparing these carbon materials through biomass pyrolysis. It introduces the unique properties and applications of these materials and explores how template synthesis, metal/heteroatom doping, and microwave-assisted pyrolysis can influence material characteristics. Additionally, the review addresses existing challenges and outlines future research directions aimed at optimizing biomass pyrolysis pathways and maximizing the efficiency and utility of the resulting carbon materials. Finally, the potential for biomass pyrolysis to shift production from fossil-based resources to waste biomass is highlighted, supporting sustainability and the transition to renewable resources.

Highly corrosive to equipment and environmentally polluting.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The production of high-value carbon materials via biomass pyrolysis has gained significant attention in recent research due to its efficiency and sustainability. Unlike the resource-intensive processes for obtaining carbon materials from fossil fuels, biomass pyrolysis provides an alternative that can utilize renewable and abundant sources at lower cost. Although biomass pyrolysis often produces a broad range of products, making precise control over specific carbon materials challenging, it offers flexibility in generating various high-value materials, including activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon foams, and carbon quantum dots. This review comprehensively examines the past decade's advancements in preparing these carbon materials through biomass pyrolysis. It introduces the unique properties and applications of these materials and explores how template synthesis, metal/heteroatom doping, and microwave-assisted pyrolysis can influence material characteristics. Additionally, the review addresses existing challenges and outlines future research directions aimed at optimizing biomass pyrolysis pathways and maximizing the efficiency and utility of the resulting carbon materials. Finally, the potential for biomass pyrolysis to shift production from fossil-based resources to waste biomass is highlighted, supporting sustainability and the transition to renewable resources.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, approximately 70 % of nations and regions worldwide have committed to attaining carbon neutrality by 2050 [1]. This ambitious goal can be realized through a combination of strategies, including reducing the reliance on fossil fuels, promoting the development of clean and renewable energy sources, transforming energy systems, and establishing green and low-carbon infrastructure. These actions are crucial for mitigating CO2 emissions and advancing global carbon neutrality [2]. Biomass is a renewable and clean energy source, possessing abundant reserves, low cost, biocompatibility and biodegradability, therefore, with wide application prospects [3]. Biomass encompasses not only wood, crops, and waste plant materials but also renewable organic substances such as household waste, sewage sludge, and animal manure [4]. Pyrolysis has emerged as a pioneering area of research among various thermochemical technologies. It generates bio-oil, bio-char, and gaseous by-products from available biomass [5]. Pyrolysis technology proves invaluable in converting biomass into high-value products. However, the direct utilization of bio-oil obtained from biomass pyrolysis presents challenges due to its high oxygen content and complex composition. Further deoxygenation and refining processes are required to enhance its usability [6]. Gas separation and purification technologies are essential for enhancing the purity of hydrogen generated during the biomass pyrolysis process, which may contain impurities such as carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, methane, among others. Improving the efficiency of gas separation and purification techniques while simultaneously reducing costs and energy consumption represents a significant technological challenge [7].

Carbon materials are extensively utilized across various sectors due to their diverse properties. The 21st century has been dubbed the “Super Carbon Era,” due to the increasing reliance on carbon-based materials in energy storage, catalysis, and environmental management [8]. Biomass pyrolysis for the production of high-value carbon materials holds several advantages, most notably its ability to produce a variety of carbon materials tailored to specific applications [9]. These materials—such as activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon foams, and carbon quantum dots—share critical properties such as high surface area, tunable porosity, and abundant functional groups, which make them ideal for a wide array of applications. Specifically, these carbon materials are highly valued for their roles in energy storage (such as supercapacitors and lithium-ion batteries), catalysis (including heterogeneous catalysis), environmental remediation (adsorption and separation), and electronics (conductivity and electrochemical performance) [10–12]. These applications leverage their high surface area, electrical conductivity, and reactivity, making them integral to advancing green technologies and sustainable solutions.

While biomass pyrolysis produces a wide range of carbon materials, this is both an advantage and a challenge. Unlike the more controlled processes for obtaining carbon materials from fossil fuels, biomass pyrolysis exhibits lower selectivity, meaning the process generates a broader spectrum of products [13]. This lower selectivity, however, offers flexibility, allowing for the production of diverse types of carbon materials (e.g., activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon foam, and carbon quantum dots) [14–16]. In contrast, carbon materials derived from fossil fuels tend to have more predictable properties, as fossil sources (like coal and petroleum) are chemically more uniform. Despite the broader product range, this flexibility in biomass pyrolysis enables the production of tailored materials that can be optimized for specific applications.

Currently, research in biomass pyrolysis aimed at producing high-value carbon materials primarily focuses on the following facets: (1) Selecting and pretreating biomass feedstock to enhance both the yield and quality of resultant carbon materials. (2) Optimizing pyrolysis process parameters, encompassing temperature, reaction time, and reaction medium, to finely regulate the microstructure and pore properties of carbon materials. (3) Conducting functionalization and surface modification to augment specific properties of carbon materials, such as conductivity and catalytic activity. (4) Exploring the application potential of carbon materials across diverse domains, including supercapacitors, lithium-ion batteries, catalysts, and other areas. The present review provides an overview of the preparation of high-value carbon materials, including activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon foam, and carbon quantum dots, via biomass pyrolysis. Furthermore, methodologies such as template synthesis, metal heteroatom doping, and microwave-assisted approaches are introduced for the fabrication of these high-value carbon materials. The aim of this study is to offer guidance for the industrial-scale production of high-performance carbon materials. This research endeavors to pave the way for the large-scale manufacturing of high-performance carbon materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of high-value carbon materials from biomass

ce:section.ce:para: Activated carbon, a conventional carbon material, is extensively employed for its strong adsorption capability in gas separation, solvent retrieval, elimination of organic impurities from drinking water and as a catalyst supporter [17]. Using biomass to produce activated carbon is an efficient and environmentally friendly pollution control strategy. Firstly, it allows for the sequestration of carbon elements present in biomass. Secondly, the activated carbon produced does not pose environmental pollution concerns and can be introduced into soil, thus participating in the natural carbon cycling process [18]. By adjusting parameters such as temperature, reaction time, and reaction medium during the pyrolysis process, it is possible to effectively control the microstructure and pore characteristics of activated carbon, thereby achieving control and optimization of its performance. As the pyrolysis temperature increases, the yield of activated carbon decreases, while the specific surface area, pore volume, and adsorption capacity increase. The production of top-quality activated carbon often involves elevated temperatures and extended activation periods, causing considerable energy expenditure [19]. However, when the pyrolysis temperature surpasses the optimal range, the destruction of micropores leads to the formation of larger pores, thereby reducing the adsorption capacity. As the temperature of activation rises, the level of volatiles declines while the ignition point of the activated carbon increases.

Activation is a common method to increase the pore volume and surface area of carbon materials [14]. The majority of biomass contains low mineral content, which limits its ability to produce high-value activated carbon. Therefore, activation treatment is often required, involving the introduction of suitable activators during the pyrolysis process. The addition of an activator enhances the pore structure and specific surface area of the resulting carbon material. Activation methods can be broadly classified into physical activation and chemical activation, with a comparison of these two approaches presented in Table 1 . Activation typically results in reduced hydrogen content and facilitates the transition of molecular chains within activated carbon towards more stable carbon structures, thus maintaining consistent calorific values before and after activation [20].

The physical activation method involves exposing carbonaceous materials to oxidative gases, including oxygen, air, carbon dioxide, water vapor, and their mixtures, at high temperatures, thereby promoting pore formation through oxidation-reduction reactions. The production rate, pore dimensions and surface area of activated carbon are primarily affected by the quantity of activator, the temperature and duration of activation, the particle size of biomass, alongside other pertinent considerations. Serafin et al. [21] prepared porous activated carbon with a specific surface area of up to 1937 m2/g, exhibiting enhanced CO2 adsorption capacity and cycling stability for CO2 capture, as well as suitability for low-cost supercapacitor applications, through the physical activation of Andiroba shell hydrothermal carbon with CO2. Gao et al. [22 [][][]acquired a sturdy columnar porous carbon adsorbent, through carbonizing, steam activating, and desalinating coffee grounds via alkali washing. The adsorption capacity at equilibrium for CH4 amounted to 0.87 mmol g−1 while the selective separation capacity for CH4/N2 mixtures (3/7,vol/vol) had a value of 10.3. Niksiar et al. [23]employed a nozzle bed reactor for the pyrolysis and activation of pistachio shells, utilizing steam as the activating agent, and achieved activated carbon with a specific surface area of 2596 m2 g⁻1 under optimized conditions. Li et al. [24 [][][]found that oxygen-assisted activation during CO2 or steam activation of biomass enhances pore development, mass yield, and surface area. Fig. 1 presents partial characterization results of activated carbons prepared by various activation methods. Although physical activation is characterized by its simplicity and cost-effectiveness, the high carbonization temperature leads to the loss of volatile matter and decomposition of organic molecules. Prolonged activation time results in excessive reaction between oxidative gases and the char, further reducing the yield of activated carbon [25].

Chemical activation methods are categorized as one-step activation and two-step activation. The former includes impregnating biomass utilizing an activator, succeeded by high-temperature activation, to produce activated carbon derived from biomass. In contrast, the latter involves thermally converting biomass to biochar, and subsequently activating impregnated biochar to form biochar-derived activated carbon [26]. In contrast, activated carbon from two-step activation shows higher carbon yield and specific surface area. During chemical activation, chemical reactions within the carbon precursor lead to the formation of pores. The activator decomposes the thermally unstable C-C bonds and ether linkages in the biochar, promoting the breakdown and rearrangement of the aromatic structure, which increases the volatility of the aromatic components and creates more pores. Redox reactions between the activator and biochar, along with the release of gaseous products, further facilitate the formation of pores, thereby increasing the number of adsorption sites on the surface of the adsorbent [27]. Illingworth et al. [28] investigated the pyrolysis and chemical activation of fibrous biomass using KOH and K2CO3, revealing distinct mechanisms: KOH initially generates micropores followed by pore widening, whereas K2CO3 simultaneously creates and widens pores, resulting in different micropore volumes and pore size distributions. Zhu et al. [29] compared the morphological characteristics of lignin char before and after KOH activation (see Fig. 2 ), revealing that the addition of KOH resulted in a roughened surface and the formation of unevenly sized pores. Moreover, it decreased the graphitization degree of lignin char, leading to the formation of more pores. Abbaci et al. [30] utilized KOH as the activating agent to convert waste potato peels into activated carbon via a two-step activation process. When the biochar/KOH ratio was 1:4, the resulting activated carbon exhibited a specific surface area of approximately 2400 m2/g and a total pore volume of 1.02 cm³/g. The possible reactions that may occur during the activation process are as follows. As the impregnation ratio increases, the activated carbon yield decreases while the specific surface area, total pore volume and adsorption capacity increase. Iranvandi et al. [31]synthesized activated carbon and chemically activated it by KOH to enhance CO2 uptake activity using fruit date seeds. The activated carbon showed a 94 % increase in average CO2 capture capacity compared to the pristine adsorbents and a 67 % increase compared to commercial adsorbents. During the chemical activation process, the regulation of the pore structure of activated carbon can be achieved by regulating parameters such as activator type, concentration, activation temperature and time. Recent research developments in the chemical activation of biomass for preparing activated carbon are detailed in Table 2 .

Carbon nanotubes are quantum materials with exceptional mechanical, electrical and optical properties, widely utilized in composites, integrated circuits and photocatalysis. These materials possess excellent potential for a range of applications, given their remarkable characteristics [41]. To achieve continuous production of high-value carbon nanotubes from biomass pyrolysis, it is crucial to carefully select the appropriate biomass and control its pyrolysis direction and principle. Liu et al. [42] found that lignin pyrolysis at temperatures above 1300 °C produces H2 and CO. CO forms C-C dimers on the surface of Fe particles and synthesizes multi-walled carbon nanotubes in a “cap-like” growth mode on the catalyst surface. The synthesis and structure of carbon nanotubes are shown in Fig. 3 . Li et al. [43] prepared Fe/N double-doped carbon nanotubes with good redox activity and stability by pyrolysis of lignin as raw material. Several studies have demonstrated the relatively high catalytic efficiency of Fe, Co, and Ni as catalysts in the production of carbon nanotubes [44,45]. Their presence can promote the secondary cleavage of furan, resulting in the production of monocyclic or polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, which are then dehydroaromatized on the catalyst surface to form carbon nanotubes [46]. The formation process of carbon nanotubes relies on the catalyst as a carrier, which determines the number of layers and diameter of the nanotubes. Moreover, the purity and graphitization of carbon nanotubes increase with the growth time.

In comparison, plastic waste pyrolysis also provides an ample carbon source for CNT synthesis, leveraging carbon radicals generated during high-temperature decomposition. Carbon nanotube yield and quality may be impacted by plastic type, reaction temperature, reaction pressure and heating rate. Yao et al. [47] utilized a two-stage fixed-bed reaction system with loaded Ni and Fe catalysts to catalyze the pyrolysis of plastics. TEM images of carbon deposits on Ni-Fe/γ-Al2O3 catalysts at different temperatures revealed bamboo-like, straight, and crooked filamentous carbon nanotubes at 700 °C, multiwalled carbon nanotubes at 800 °C, and encapsulating carbons with larger metal particle size at 900 °C, suggesting temperature-dependent formation of various carbon structures, as shown in Fig. 4 . In another study by Yao et al., it was found that the diameter of carbon nanotubes corresponds to the size of encapsulated catalyst particles, indicating a relationship between nanotube morphology and catalyst particle size. Additionally, a higher Ni/Fe ratio enhances the interaction between metal and support, resulting in longer and more homogeneous carbon nanotubes [48]. The bimetallic catalytic system can significantly enhance the catalyst's structural stability at elevated reaction temperatures and regulate the binding energy between the catalyst and carbon [49]. Cai et al. [11] conducted pyrolysis of plastic waste on Fe-Al2O3 to produce carbon nanotubes. They subsequently heated Fe-CNTs and melamine under an inert atmosphere to obtain an electrocatalyst. The electrocatalyst exhibited a starting wave potential of 0.943 V and a half-wave potential of 0.811 V when compared with reversible hydrogen electrode. It also displayed superior stability and anti-poisoning properties. Ramzan et al. [50] compared carbon nanotubes generated from high-density plastic waste using single-step microwave pyrolysis catalyzed by Al2O3, Fe2O3, and NiFe2O4, respectively. They observed that NiFe2O4 catalysts produced comparatively high-quality and well-structured carbon nanotubes. Biomass-derived CNTs often have a unique advantage due to the functional doping of heteroatoms (e.g., nitrogen from lignin), which enhances their catalytic activity and electrochemical applications. However, plastic pyrolysis offers a simplified process with less catalyst separation, enabling purer CNTs with fewer impurities.

In co-pyrolysis of plastics and biomass, plastics can bolster the inherent high O/C and low H/C of biomass due to their high effective hydrogen to carbon (H/C) ratio and relatively low effective oxygen to carbon (O/C) ratio. This ultimately enhances the quality and homogeneity of the products [51]. Xu et al. [52] investigated the co-pyrolysis characteristics of biomass and plastic waste and identified that an increased plastic content facilitated the formation of carbon nanotubes featuring long, straight walls and a uniform diameter. Song et al. [53] conducted a study on the co-pyrolysis characteristics of biomass and polypropylene with Ni-Mo-Mg catalyst. Their findings indicate that the addition of biomass as a carbon precursor had a certain impact on the morphology of carbon nanotubes. Compared to individual pyrolysis of either biomass or plastics, the co-pyrolysis of both materials enhances the quality of carbon nanotube production.

Graphene, characterized by its two-dimensional lattice structure comprising carbon atoms arranged in sp2 hybridization, exhibits exceptional properties, including high electron mobility, thermal conductivity, and mechanical strength. These remarkable features make graphene highly versatile and suitable for a wide range of applications in fields such as electronics, optics, thermodynamics, and mechanics [54]. The hierarchical structure of graphene exhibits pores of different length scales, with specific functions in energy storage. Effective adjustment of the specific surface area and structural properties can be achieved through pyrolysis temperature manipulatio [55]. Long et al. [56] first transformed sugarcane fibers into cellulose-based two-dimensional crystals, followed by carbonization and graphitization to obtain graphene. The resulting carbon product was identified as multi-layer graphene using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). A schematic diagram of the graphene synthesis process and SEM/HRTEM images of the carbon product are shown in Fig. 5 . Liu et al. [78] synthesized hierarchically graded porous graphene from lotus leaves using a natural calcium oxide in situ autocatalytic method. The resulting graphene displays a three-dimensional structural hierarchy comprising microscopic in-plane vacancies, mesoscopic pores and macroscopic cavities. The material's outstanding properties include rapid charge transfer, short ion diffusion paths and a sturdy structure. The folded layered structure of graphene augmented with an increase in holding time. Active sites for the growth of graphene layers are surface defects on metal nanoparticles and metal oxides. During the process of catalytic synthesis for graphene structures, the graphene layer grows alongside the crystal particle edges, resulting in efficient catalytic activity for the formation of graphene [57]. Esteve-Adell et al. [58] prepared nitrogen-doped graphene thin films on nano-hexagonal boron nitride flakes in a single step by pyrolysis of chitosan containing about 20 wt% ammonium borate as precursor. The introduction of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, boron and phosphorus into the graphene structure improves the electrochemical and oxygen reduction properties [85].

Foam carbon is a porous carbon material with an interconnected three-dimensional microstructure. Although both foam carbon and activated carbon are porous carbon materials, activated carbon is typically characterized by a high surface area and a well-developed micropore network, making it highly suitable for adsorption applications [59]. In contrast, foam carbon has a more complex, interconnected three-dimensional structure comprising both macropores and micropores, which imparts distinctive mechanical and thermal properties [60]. foam carbon boasts high porosity, strength, and low density, as well as excellent adiabatic and insulating properties, making it extremely corrosion resistant. It is presently utilized as an adsorbent, an electrode, a catalyst carrier, and a thermal insulator at high temperatures [61]. Foam carbon produced through conventional means has limited mechanical and dimensional stability, and a complex production process. Therefore, in recent years, researchers have suggested producing foam carbon by pyrolyzing specific biomass materials [62]. This technique embodies an economical and uncomplicated procedure. The pyrolysis conditions and choice of feedstock greatly influence the porosity, mechanical strength, and surface chemistry of foam carbon. By adjusting the pyrolysis environment and processing techniques, the composition and properties of foam carbon can be further tailored. Unlike activated carbon, foam carbon is characterized by a hierarchical pore structure with interconnected macropores, enhancing its ability to facilitate rapid ion migration and increasing its effective surface area for energy storage applications. Zhang et al. [63] used the autofoaming method to produce foamy carbon from palm kernel shells. The resulting carbon foam exhibits a hierarchical pore structure, comprising multilayered micro-, meso-, and macropores, along with increased sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The synthesis process, self-foaming and evolution of the chemical structure of this foamy carbon is shown in Fig. 6 . Ozbay et al. [64] liquefied spruce tree sawdust using a mixture of bio-oil and phenol as a solvent, followed by slow pyrolysis. Foamed carbon was observed to have a smaller average bubble size and a more homogeneous bubble structure in comparison to resin foams. Palazzolo et al. [65] conducted a pyrolysis of calcium lignosulfonate as a feedstock in an N2 atmosphere. They found that pyrolysis at lower N2 pressures effectively controlled the thermal decomposition of the precursor, resulting in the production of carbon foams with a single open macroporous cell. Rodriguez et al. [66] explored the co-pyrolysis behavior of coal and biomass, revealing that the addition of biomass improved the porosity of the resulting carbon foam. Stahlfeld et al. [67] explored the properties of foamy carbon derived from corn stover pyrolysis under elevated pressure conditions. They noted that the genesis of carbon foam stemmed from the desolvation process at heightened pressures. As pressure rose, the mass yield of carbon foam increased, albeit at the expense of reduced density and ultimate compressive strength. Fu et al. [68] employed varying amounts of KOH to catalytically pyrolyze rice husk granules (RHP), resulting in the formation of layered foam-like porous carbon structures. In comparison with RHP-Char, the addition of KOH leads to carbon products with tailored porosity and enhanced pore distribution. The surface microstructure of these carbon products is illustrated in Fig. 7 . During the pyrolysis of biomass, oxygen-containing functional groups release CO, H2, CO2, CH4, H2O and volatile compounds. As a consequence, there is a reduction in the carbon mass within the produced foam, resulting in expansion of bubble volume and progressive thinning of bubble walls, ultimately culminating in the formation of a foam structure [69]. Foam carbon offers numerous advantages, including low density, excellent mechanical properties, and superior thermal insulation, all of which are crucial for specialized industrial applications.

Carbon quantum dots(CQDs) have garnered substantial interest as a novel variation of carbon nanomaterials due to their adaptable optical properties, favorable hydrophilicity, exceptional chemical stability, and heightened conductivity [70]. In recent years, CQDs have shown significant potential in fields such as heavy metal ion detection and optoelectronic devices. However, traditional methods for producing CQDs often require large amounts of solvents, leading to low atom utilization and environmental concerns. These methods also suffer from limitations in spectral efficiency range, low product yield, and difficulty in large-scale synthesis. Green and high-quality bio-based carbon quantum dots can be generated by direct pyrolysis of biomass. Additionally, CQDs manufactured through biomass pyrolysis illustrate various desirable properties, including low cost, simplicity of production, high yield, excellent optical attributes, biocompatibility, and stability [71]. Agnol et al. [72] synthesized CQDs via the pyrolysis of spirulina, observing the highest quantum yield (23.5 %) at relatively short durations and higher temperatures. Under visible and ultraviolet light irradiation, the CQDs exhibited pale yellow and intense blue fluorescence, respectively. Wang et al. [73] synthesized CQDs from wool using a one-step microwave pyrolysis process, achieving a yield of 16.3 %. This approach is simple and does not require additives such as acids, bases, or salts, eliminating the need for complex post-treatment procedures. Song et al. [74] produced solid CQDs from collagen waste by calcination. The resulting carbon dots are an excellent sensor platform for Fe3+ in acidic environments with strong immunity to interference. Ganesan et al. [75] developed self-assembled multifunctional CQDs through a direct pyrolysis method, combined with microwave-assisted synthesis, using biomass as the precursor. The synthesized CQDs are spherical particles with an average diameter of approximately 2.4 nm, exhibiting high crystallinity akin to graphene (Fig. 8 a and b). Additionally, under sunlight and ultraviolet ray(UV) irradiation, the CQD solutions display light yellow and bluish-green colors, respectively, maintaining photostability even after 100 min of UV exposure (Fig. 8c and d). Different pyrolysis temperatures have been found to influence the quantum yield, surface state, and degree of graphitization of CQDs, leading to distinct fluorescence behaviors of the dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods to enhance the performance of pyrolytic carbon material

ce:section.ce:para: The templating method employs template agents to create porous carbon with a regularly ordered structure and uniform pore size distribution. During the charring process, this template improves both the physical structure of the biomass material (hierarchical pores) and its surface chemistry (N-O functional groups) [76]. Nitrogen-containing functional groups can increase the polarity of the carbon surface, facilitating interactions with target molecules. In contrast, oxygen-containing functional groups enhance surface acidity and promote the adhesion of hydrophilic volatile organic compounds to the carbon surface [77,78]. The methodology can utilize a “self-template” present in the biomass source or an “external template” added later. Qin et al. [79] discovered that in-situ templates in the synthesis of porous carbons facilitate the formation of diverse pore structures, with the highest surface area observed for pig manure-derived activated carbon after hydrofluoric acid washing (2321 m2/g). This is attributed to the synergistic effect of various template compounds, further activated by hydrofluoric acid pickling to maximize their role in pore formation. Kelp harbors numerous inorganic salts such as potassium, magnesium, calcium, sodium, and others. These salts can act as self-templates while carbonizing, fortifying the pore structure and facilitating the creation of a porous framework [80]. Li et al. [81] identified a correlation between the amount of template and the specific surface area and porosity of porous carbon. In cases of excessive template, the formation of large pores results in a decrease in the specific surface area of the carbon material. Conversely, a lack of template leads to incomplete pore structures within the carbon material. Zhang et al. [82] compared the porous carbon yield and adsorption capacity of heavy bio-oil with calcium carbonate, calcium oxalate and calcium citrate as templating agents at different mass ratios. It was found that the adsorption capacity reaches a maximum of 310 mg g−1 at a calcium citrate mass ratio of 1:2. Zhang et al. [83] found that biomass pyrolysis steam undergoes complex reactions with template agents to form biochar matrices, with oxygen-containing functional groups formed on their surfaces. Moreover, excessively high pyrolysis temperatures can limit the promoting effect of templates on the formation of pore structures in carbon precursors. Fig. 9 illustrates the potential pore formation process of porous carbon synthesized from cotton/polyester textile waste (CPW) using calcium acetate (CA) as a template agent. When employing inorganic non-carbonizing salts as templating agents, elevating the temperature results in the presence of molten salts, inducing condensation of the carbon source and formation of a superior carbon skeleton. While template synthesis can produce carbon materials with uniform pore sizes, the subsequent post-treatment process is often complex and may contribute to significant environmental pollution.

Doping biomass pyrolysis-derived carbon materials with metals or heteroatoms can significantly enhance their performance by improving electrical conductivity, catalytic activity, surface area, porosity, mechanical strength, tunability of electronic properties, and chemical stability. These enhancements are crucial for applications in energy storage, catalysis, gas storage, separation, environmental remediation, and advanced electronics. Xu et al. [85] found that doping biochar with Fe, Ni, and Zn resulted in a distinct layered pore structure and noticeable lattice fringes, which significantly enhanced the degree of graphitization in the biochar. Xu et al. [86 [][][]obtained porous carbon materials with different doping metal atoms and pore structures by carbonizing plasma and alkaline metal salt mixtures, demonstrating excellent electrocatalytic activity. Gunasekaran et al. [87] utilized urea as a nitrogen source to synthesize bamboo-based N-doped activated carbon. The resulting material exhibited a porous wrinkled nanosheet morphology and demonstrated excellent electrochemical performance. Nie et al. [88 [][][]synthesized nitrogen-doped layered porous carbon (NxPCy) using corn stover as the carbon source and NH4Cl as the nitrogen source. By finely tuning the pore structure and elemental ratios, the N2PC5 electrode demonstrated outstanding supercapacitive performance, attaining a specific capacitance of 321.5 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1. The incorporation of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur significantly enhances the doping efficiency and performance of metal-doped carbon structures. Wang et al. [89] discovered that the introduction of ammonium tetrathiomolybdate during the pyrolysis of ginkgo leaves enhances the specific surface area of N, S-doped carbon materials. The Mo nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed on the surface of the carbon sheets, demonstrating excellent hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity. Specifically, heteroatom doping improves metal dispersion by creating active functional sites on the carbon surface, which interact effectively with metal precursors, resulting in uniformly distributed metal nanoparticles and mitigating aggregation [90]. Additionally, heteroatom doping alters the electronic structure of the carbon matrix, enhancing metal-carbon interactions and boosting catalytic activity and stability through electron donor effects. This is particularly advantageous for electrocatalytic reactions like oxygen reduction (ORR) and HER. Hao et al. [91] found that in situ self-doped N, S, Mo and Fe carbon materials extracted from legume rhizomes exhibited excellent ORR activity and stability, which was mainly attributed to the synergistic effect between in situ bimetallic (Mo and Fe) and heteroatomic (N and S) self-doping. Moreover, heteroatoms provide additional active sites and facilitate synergistic catalytic effects between metal particles and the carbon matrix, enhancing reaction rates and selectivity in both heterogeneous and electrocatalytic processes. Structural stability is also improved, as heteroatoms like nitrogen form strong metal-nitrogen bonds, stabilizing metal nanoparticles and extending catalyst lifespan. Fig. 10 shows the schematic of the synthesis of metal doped and metal/heteroatom co-doped carbon materials, respectively.

Microwave-assisted pyrolysis has emerged as an effective method for enhancing the properties of carbon-based materials, providing significant advantages over traditional heating methods, such as faster heating rates, lower energy consumption, simpler control, minimal thermal inertia, and targeted heating efficiency [93]. The microwave radiation during pyrolysis facilitates the release of volatile components, which stimulates the activation stage of biomass, leading to the formation of carbon materials with improved properties [94]. Duran-Jimenez et al. [95 [][][]found that sustainable microwave thermal treatment enables the efficient production of ultra-microporous activated carbons from biomass waste, which exhibit high CO2 uptake and selectivity, as well as various potentials for industrial applications. Lam et al. [96] prepared activated carbon with a highly porous structure, with a fixed carbon content of up to 83 wt%, and a specific surface area of 1350 m2 g−1, by a combination of chemical impregnation and microwave-assisted pyrolysis. Dou et al. [97]discovered that microwave-assisted KOH activation enhances the performance of microalgae porous carbons, improving desulfurization efficiency and achieving efficient Hg0 capture. Zhang et al. [98] utilized microwave pyrolysis of chili straw to prepare porous carbon, revealing that microwave irradiation led to the formation of ultra-thin lamellar carbon structures on the surface of porous char. They observed that microwave irradiation facilitated the development of micropores, significantly enhancing the specific surface area of porous carbon (2768.52 m2/g). As microwave power increases, the yield of porous carbon decreases, while the specific surface area continues to increase. Numerous studies have demonstrated that microwave-assisted pyrolysis promotes the creation of carbon nanotubes. Zhang et al. [99] employed a microwave plasma discharge method to pyrolyze plastic, resulting in carbon nanotubes with an inner diameter of 5.4 nm and an outer diameter of 13.92 nm. They discovered that the morphological characteristics of the carbon nanotubes are influenced by the coupling effect between the microwave plasma and the catalyst. Baghel et al. [100] used a microwave reactor to heat a mixture of biochar and ferrocene. They found that high microwave pyrolysis temperatures helped to obtain carbon nanotubes with higher concentration, higher graphitization, good quality, high crystallinity, and fewer defects. The formation of carbon nanotubes using microwave-assisted pyrolysis is determined by several factors, including microwave radiation, the formation of hotspots, and the composition of volatiles and inorganics [101]. The rotation of carbon molecule dipoles induced by microwave energy (as illustrated in Fig. 11 ) facilitates the formation of more abundant and orderly pore structures in carbon materials, thereby enhancing their adsorption and catalytic performance [102]. This method offers an efficient and controllable means to optimize the synthesis and properties of carbon-based materials, particularly in the context of advanced energy storage, catalysis, and environmental applications [103].

Biomass pyrolysis has recently emerged as a technique for synthesizing carbon materials using biomass as a low-cost carbon source. This process can be optimized by adjusting pyrolysis parameters to achieve carbon materials with desirable yield and properties. This review summarizes the advancements in the preparation of various carbon materials over the past decades, including activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon foams, and carbon quantum dots. Furthermore, it details the methods to enhance their performance through templating, metal/heteroatom doping, and microwave-assisted pyrolysis. Despite the progress, there is still an insufficient understanding of the processes involved in producing carbon materials from biomass pyrolysis, particularly in identifying suitable biomass feedstocks and catalysts to achieve the desired structures and properties of carbon materials. This review addresses these challenges and provides insights into potential solutions and future outlooks:
1)
Biomass has diverse compositions, necessitating the classification and concentration of biomass components. Unique biomass categories can yield distinctive groups of carbon materials with unique properties.
2)
To deepen the understanding of pyrolysis mechanisms, the pyrolysis behavior and kinetics of biomass will be studied. Thermogravimetric analysis combined with infrared spectroscopy will be used to investigate the pyrolysis behavior and kinetics of biomass. The ultimate goal of this research is to explore how changing pyrolysis process conditions can enhance the value of carbon materials.
3)
Biomass-derived carbon materials are renewable, easy to process, have tunable surface properties, and are relatively low-cost. To produce high-quality carbon materials on a large industrial scale more economically and efficiently, it is essential to study the mechanisms by which raw materials and various catalysts affect the structure and performance of carbon materials.

In conclusion, this review envisions the future trajectory of biomass pyrolysis for the synthesis of high-value carbon materials, emphasizing the transition from fossil-based resources to waste biomass utilization.

Pan Li: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Yasen Chen: Conceptualization. Yucheng Lin: Data curation. Wei Chen: Investigation. Junhao Hu: Investigation. Wei Yang: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Chun Chang: Resources. Shusheng Pang: Writing – review & editing.
